nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to ancient lakes and connected rivers of central and southeast Sulawesi — primarily Lake Poso and the Malili lake system (Lakes Matano, Towuti, Mahalona, Lontoa, Masapi) — on hard rocky, sandy, and woody substrates in clear, warm, hard alkaline water.

decorPreferences: cuttlebone or other calcium source to support shell

diet.notes: Generally considered a slow grazer of biofilm, soft algae, and detritus; readily takes sinking algae wafers, blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini, cucumber), and high-quality sinking pellets. Has been reported to nibble on soft new growth of some live plants (e.g. Anubias leaves) when underfed.

goodWith: Sulawesi Caridina shrimp (Cardinal, Harlequin, Goldflake) at matched hard alkaline parameters
small peaceful rasboras and tetras tolerating warm, hard, alkaline water
Nerite, Mystery, and other peaceful community snails
avoidWith: Assassin Snails (Clea helena) and other snail-eating predators
loaches and pufferfish that hunt snails
large or aggressive cichlids and crayfish

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Bloodshot20; 1183x652. Vibrant golden/orange rabbit-snail morph with bright yellow body, extended foot and long whiskered tentacles against a dark tapered shell — sharp and clearly shows the diagnostic rabbit-like face and coloration.

Photo by Bschlund; 3733x2489. Crisp in-tank shot of the dark 'chocolate' morph (T. towutensis) showing the long ribbed conical shell and extended body with tentacle — adds color/species variety to the umbrella entry.

summary: The Rabbit Snail (Tylomelania zemis) is a slow-grazing freshwater snail from the ancient lakes of Sulawesi, recognizable by its long, tapered shell and whiskered, rabbit-like face. It thrives in warm, hard, alkaline water and is generally considered peaceful, pairing well with Sulawesi Caridina shrimp.
careNotes:
Umbrella entry covering the entire Tylomelania trade group: Tylomelania zemis (the most commonly sold 'Chocolate' / Poso form), T. patriarchalis, T. gemmifera (the 'Golden/Orange' Matano form), and a range of Tylomelania spp. sold under trade-morph names — Yellow, Orange, Chocolate, Gold Spot, Spotted Black, White Spotted, and Yellow Spotted Rabbit Snails. All of these are endemic to the same Sulawesi ancient-lake system and have effectively identical husbandry needs, so their care needs are effectively interchangeable.

Water chemistry is the make-or-break variable. Tylomelania evolved in the hard, alkaline, mineral-rich waters of Lake Poso and the Malili lakes, and they are commonly considered intolerant of soft or acidic water — shell pitting and erosion appear quickly under low GH/KH. Generally advised to keep in the upper end of the hardness range (GH 6–15, KH 4–10) and to provide a continuous calcium source such as cuttlebone, crushed coral in the filter, or aragonite-bearing substrate. Stable temperature in the 24–29 °C range and clean water (low nitrate, no detectable ammonia/nitrite) round out the recipe.

Temperament is reliably peaceful; they are slow, conspicuous grazers that wander day and night and don't bother tankmates. They pair beautifully with Sulawesi Caridina shrimp (Cardinal, Harlequin, Yellow Goldflake), which want the same warm, hard, alkaline parameters. They are not lid-jumpers in the dramatic mystery-snail sense, but they have been known to climb above the waterline and out of open-topped tanks, so a tight-fitting lid is generally advised.

Reproduction is ovoviviparous (live-bearing): adults release single, fully-formed, ~1–2 cm juveniles rather than egg clutches, and they do so very slowly — only a handful of offspring per year per female. Populations almost never explode in the way pond/bladder/Malaysian trumpet snail populations do. Sexes are separate (dioecious), so a group of 3+ is needed for any chance of breeding.
breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous live-bearers with separate sexes (dioecious). Females release one to a few large, fully-formed juveniles at a time — among the largest newly-hatched freshwater gastropods at ~1–2 cm shell length. Reproduction is very slow, so population control is rarely an issue. A group of 3+ adults at stable warm, hard, alkaline parameters with abundant food has been reported to produce occasional offspring; targeted breeding is uncommon in the hobby.

primary.name: The Shrimp Farm
primary.notes: Designated primary source. WebFetch returned HTTP 403 on the root domain and on the dedicated Chocolate Rabbit Snail (Tylomelania zemis) care article (https://www.theshrimpfarm.com/posts/chocolate-rabbit-snail-care/) and the Golden Rabbit Snail care article. Information about The Shrimp Farm's Tylomelania catalog was confirmed via Google search snippets only.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Designated secondary. Confirmed Tylomelania sp., ~4 in (10 cm) adult size, temp 76–82°F, pH 7.0–7.8, Sulawesi (Lake Poso) origin, peaceful temperament, slow live-bearing reproduction, omnivorous grazing.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Tylomelania
additional.notes: Taxonomic and native-range authority: family Pachychilidae, endemic to Sulawesi (Lake Poso and Malili lake system: Matano, Towuti, Lontoa, Mahalona, Masapi), ovoviviparous, dioecious, newly-hatched juveniles ~2 cm. Confirmed wild water conditions pH 7.3–8.5 and 78–84°F.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World — Complete Guide to Rabbit Snail Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked parameters: adult size ~7.6 cm, temp 20–30°C, pH 7.3–8.5, KH 2–15, peaceful, omnivorous, calcium important. (This source incorrectly describes the genus as egg-laying; Wikipedia and aqua-trade sources confirm ovoviviparous — Wikipedia followed.)

additional.name: FishLab — Rabbit Snail Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked: T. zemis is the most common species in trade, up to 10 cm, 24–29°C, pH 7.5–8.5, GH 3–10, KH 2–8, lifespan 2–4 years, live-bearing, calcium supplementation required, climbs out of open tanks.

additional.name: AquariumNexus — Rabbit Snail Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked hard-water requirement (GH 6–12 typical, some sources higher; KH 2–6 typical with calcium supplementation strongly recommended) and Lake Poso / Lake Matano native habitat.
